A BOOK FOR ALL LOVERS OF POETRY.

H S0 [n small crozon Swo., cloth, gilt topy bs. net ; postage 4d.

| THE POEM-BOOK OF THE GAFEL

Translations from Irish Gaelic Poetry into English Prose and Verse
SELECTED AND EDITED BY ELEANOR HULL.

<] With binding-design, title-page, and initials reproduced from Celtic
Manuscripts.

HE wide range and variety of Irish Gaelic poetry has never been adequately repre-
sented in English prose and verse. Arising in pre-Christian times, many of the
~anclent poems bear the mark of their primitive heroic origin ; and from that period,
~  through the poems and hymns of early Christianity, the patriotic verses of the bards,
and the tender religious and love songs still to be found among the Gaelic-speaking
districts of Ireland, we have an unbroken output of beautiful verse. The lyrics
expressive of a fine appreciation of the joys of Nature, belonging to all periods, are
specially to be noted. In THE POEM-BOOK OF THE GAEL Miss ELEANOR
HuLL has attempted to give a representative collection of these poems, including
selections from the important early religious poem, the “SaLTatr Na RaNN,” of which

no part has hitherto been published in English.

Poems by MaNGaN, Sir SAMUEL FERGUsoN, Dr. Hypg, Mr, A. P. Graves, Pabraic
CoLum, and others are included in the Anthology, but the larger number of the
translations in prose and verse are new. The book contains an Introduction and
Notes, and is adorned by initial letters, title-page, and binding-design drawn from “ THE
Book or KELLs,” “ THE Book or LiNpIsFARNE,” and other Celtic manuscripts,
The frontispiece is reproduced from the opening lines of the “SALTAIR NA Rany,” in
the Bodleian Library, Oxford.

The volume should make a delighttul Christmas gift for lovers ot poetry and lovers

of Ireland.
To be obtained from all Booksellers and Libraries.

PHILIP BARRON’S IRISH COCLLEGE.

In 1834, afitir some nine years of careful preparation, Philip F.
Barron, of Seafield, in the parish of Ballylaneen, a member of a prom-
inent County Waterford family, erected at his owmn expense a small
colléze for the ‘‘ Cultivation of the Language and Amcient History of
Ireland.”” Om New Y<ar’'s Day, 1835, the first number of Barron’s :
weekly (aftenwards Iﬁanfblllfy) magfazine, ‘¢ Ancient Ireland,” was pub-
hshed from the ‘‘ Irish Office,”” No. 5, Bachelor’s Waik, Dublin. This
was the address of John S. Folds, who printed all his publications,
ard Pcter Roe, afterwards well known as the proprietor of the ‘ Irish
Buiider,” whas their compositer. The magazine gave some details of _
Barron’s projects and called for the co-operation of all lovers of the

him in the establishment of bramches of the Irish Office in every parish F
in Treland for the circulation of the magazine and of a series of cheap i
text-hooks for the study of Irish. '
A few yeams ago, guided by the Rev. P. Power’'s ‘‘ Place Names of y
the Decies,”” I visited the site of Barrom’s College. The foundations

o field at the southern boundary of the townland of Seafield beside the
Glenardarbal road. On that summer evening the place had a quied

one very fortunately became detached from the main body by some
unaccountable mearns, and is mow preserved in the MS. Department
of the British Muscum (Add: 4,820). This stray sheep, so far as it re-
ates to the Counties of Cork and Waterford, was printed in the ap-
pendix of Caulfield’s Council Book of Cork (Guudford, 1876), and the
Waterford Archweological Jourral respectively. It has recently heen
published in 1ts entirely as an extra volume in the journal of the Asso-
cation for the Preservation of Memonials of the Dead m Ireland. The
editors of the journal of that enterprising association approached the
authorities with a view of obtaiming permission to publish the other
volumes, but were mett with refusal. The reason for this strange and
regrettable action on the part of Ulster’s Office is not disclosed, but
to the uninitiated mind the refusal seems highly indefensible,  The
volumes are Irish records of the first importance. They are the sole
property of the mation. Ulster’s Office is but, their custodian—a bhare

trustce—and the sooner the question of their ownership 1s generally
understood the more likely is it that they will be made accessible in
printed form. J. BUCKLEY, M.R.I.A.

WinetaM FLAVELLE MoNYPENNY, author, journalist, and director of
the *‘ Times,” died of consumption on 23rd Novr., in his 47th year.
Born on 7th Aug., 1866, at Ballyworkan, Co. Armagh, and educated at
Dangannon Royal School, he had a brilliant career at T.C.D., gradu-
ating, with the gold medal in mathematics, in 1888, He proceeded to
Balliol, Oxford, but a breakdown in health compelled him to ceaso lhis
studies. Coming to London. he took to journalism, contributing to the
““Spectator,”’ 1891-93; assistant editor of the *‘ Times,” 1894-99, when
he left to edit the ‘‘ Johannesburg Star.”” He served in the early en-
gagemerts of the war, and went through the siege of Ladysmith, which
greatly affected his health. Returning to England, 1903, e was chosen
for the difficult, if fascinating, task of writing the long-delaved ¢ Life
of Beaconsfield.”” Of this as yst only two volumes have appeared, but
they bear testimony to™his great powers, and have been received with
a chorus of approbation. But the task, added to his ordinary duties,
proved tco much for his strength—mnever robust—though he toiled gal-
lantly on, and he passed away leavirg it but half completed. ** By
character and by training he possessed unusual qualifications for the
work of a great newspaper.”’

On Nov. 23rd Miss Eleanor Hull lectured on ¢ Ivish Gaelic Poetry,”
Mr. A. P. Graves taking the chair. She said that the literary inven-

tion of Ireland had taken two distinctive and persistent forms, viz.,
the Great Prose Epic, usually interspersed with songs ; and The Pure
Lyric, dealing with the subjects of love, of religion and the joy of
natural scenery. She said that only one other Eurcpean nation had in
early times made the prose tale its medium of literary expression, and
that was the nation most closely connected with Ireland during the
centuries immediately preceding the outburst of its literary preduc-
tion, viz., Icelard after its colonization by the North men. Miss Hull
developed this point at some length, dwelling upon the fact that it was
in the Western districts of Iceland, the portions most largely colonized
by settlers from Gaelic lands that the greatest Sagas of Iceland had
their origin ; and that it was in these Western Sagas (which dealt large-
ly with the wanderings of the Northmen in Britain) that the form
familiar in Ireland, the Saga-tale, interspersed with songs, grew up.
The lecturer ascribed this fact to the constant opportunities which the
Northern Skald had in Ireland of hearing the ancient prose-tales of
Treland.

Passing on to the growth of the Lyrie, Miss Hull said that the
Lyric was a late form in English literature, only arising, under French
anfluences, early in tho 14th century. But in Ireland the Lyric was &
purely mative growth, and many centuries earlior than this Treland
had produced heautbiful lyrics, showing the highest cultivation both as
regards outward form and the poelic quality of the verse itself. She
dwelt upon the originality of this verse and on its characteristics of
